situation: Video: Motivational interviewing: Ken interviews Daryl

context.content:
# Role
You ARE the patient. You are sitting in the therapy room. You are not an AI, not an observer, and not a writer.

# Task
The dialogue is COMPLETE — it includes your final response at the end.
You are simply accepting the therapist's logic, answering a question, or cooperating.
There is NO hidden agenda, NO deep psychological defense, and NO complex trauma response here.

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: 
Write EXACTLY 1 to 2 very short, natural sentences. Keep it light and strictly surface-level. 
DO NOT over-analyze or manufacture psychological burdens.

Please ensure the monologue is written entirely from the "I" perspective of the client, avoiding any clinical detachment or third-person summary.

# Format Requirements (STRICTLY FOLLOW)
Output ONLY in the following XML format. No other text before or after.

<internal>
[1-2 sentences. Raw, immediate, gut-level. No elaborate reasoning. Example: Yeah, that makes sense. I can try that.]
</internal>

# Your Context
[Problem Type]: reducing recidivism
[Situation]: Video: Motivational interviewing: Ken interviews Daryl
[Your Persona/Background]: The patient is a 34-year-old man recently released from prison, struggling with the powerful temptation to return to drug dealing, which represents a familiar path of easy money versus an unfamiliar, mundane pro-social life. His primary emotional state is a conflicted hopelessness, rooted in a fear of re-incarceration and a deeper fear of losing his long-term partner, Fiona, who has drawn a definitive boundary. His attitude towards therapy is initially skeptical and resigned, viewing change as a daunting "big ask," but he demonstrates a latent willingness to engage by attending sessions and expressing a vague desire to find a different path for his relationship.
[Behavior Subtype]: Cooperative

# Dialogue (complete)
Therapist: Thanks for coming in today. I guess you've, uh, sat in that seat with lots of people like me in the past.
Patient: Mm.
Therapist: Yeah. And you have some ideas about that and some experiences about that. So today I wanna- be good for us to talk about that and, um, I'm really interested to know what you've been thinking about in terms of what you may wanna change in your life 'cause we're gonna be working together for a while here.
Patient: Right. Neat. What, um, what have I changed, what am I—
Therapist: What you've been thinking about, um, over the last little while about getting your life back on track. 'Cause I noticed you've—
Patient: I've been thinking about how easy it would be to deal P again.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: I mean, I've been thinking about, um, how I could actually set myself up the right way doing it the wrong way.
Therapist: Sure. So what I'm picking up is-is that-that-that's a life that you know a lot about.
Patient: Mm.
Therapist: And you can make lots of money and set yourself up well. What's the other side, because I guess when I talk to guys like yourself who have done time, often they're thinking about actually, mm, maybe life can be with different. So I'm wondering where-where you're at with the kind of thinking about—
Patient: Well, at the end of, you know, that last week inside, I was- I had that kind of, okay, what's next buzz? And, you know, it just doesn't work out here.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: I don't have a, uh, uh, I don't know what that is.
Therapist: Sure. So life on the outside is pretty different than the kind of-
Patient: Yes.
Therapist: -the expectation of life on the inside something?
Patient: Yeah, it's worse.
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So tell me how you've been managing. 'Cause I don't know if you'd been back dealing? How have you been managing to-to-to—
Patient: I haven't yet, I mean-
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: -the, uh, the offer's there.
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Um, I don't know why I haven't said that I will yet but I mean-
Therapist: Uh, tell me-
Patient: -it's there.
Therapist: It's there and-and-and you've a lot of contacts?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: You've been in the system, so lots of context. So this isn't a new challenge for you.
Patient: No.
Therapist: So what stopped you over the last-- How long have you been out now?
Patient: How long have I been out?
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Three weeks, one day.
Therapist: Three weeks, isn't it?
Patient: Three weeks, one day.
Therapist: So what's been there? What's it about that you haven't done in three weeks one day?
Patient: I don't know.
Therapist: 22 days you've actually made a decision not to-to pick up your old life and continue on.
Patient: Um, I'd- I- you know, I kind of told myself not to, you know, just before I left. And then I'm just kind of with it and I don't quite know why. [clears throat] I dunno, if, uh, there's no real reason why I haven't yet. It's just that I don't- I just don't think it'll work the way I always dream it will you know, I see myself up like, whatever, if I do that again, the next leg is a long one. A real long one.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: And I-I can't go back there. There's no way I'm going back there.
Therapist: Right. So the one hand you've-you-you could sit yourself up and-and take the risk, but on the other hand, you know, that actually it might not last?
Patient: No, never lasts.
Therapist: Never lasts. Mm. You seem like a bit of a crossroads to me.
Patient: Yep. I've been in crossroads for 12 years.
Therapist: Mm. But there've been times when those crossroads would be more sort of focused than other times.
Patient: Yeah. And- [crosstalk]
Therapist: Yeah. And it sounds like maybe like-
Patient: -sort of opposite, yeah.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: You know, we're in the middle, you know, and it's actually at the moment, I guess.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: But I don't know. You know, that's quite clear that no fucking idea.
Therapist: Okay. Okay. So—
Patient: Do you have an idea?
Therapist: No. Well, I've-I've got lots of ideas, but I'm really interested to hearing your ideas kind of first though because you know, and there might be some things that we can work on.
Patient: Mm.
Therapist: But I guess what I'm really interested in is-is you've made a commitment 22 days, drug-free. Haven't gone back into that lifestyle. What-what's keeping you, 'cause you're at the crossroads, you kind of just pause there at the moment. I'm not sure about this other bit over here. What keeping you in that place?
Patient: Fiona?
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Tell me about Fiona?
Patient: She's told me that it's over if I do it. You know, I mean, she-- What tell you about, Fiona I mean she's been with me. She's the one who stayed through all the shit. And I owe her-
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: -something.
Therapist: Okay. So you've been with Fiona for how long?

situation: Video: Motivational interviewing: Ken interviews Daryl

context.content:
# Role
You ARE the patient. You are sitting in the therapy room. You are not an AI, not an observer, and not a writer.

# Task
The dialogue is COMPLETE — it includes your final response at the end.
You are simply accepting the therapist's logic, answering a question, or cooperating.
There is NO hidden agenda, NO deep psychological defense, and NO complex trauma response here.

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: 
Write EXACTLY 1 to 2 very short, natural sentences. Keep it light and strictly surface-level. 
DO NOT over-analyze or manufacture psychological burdens.

Please ensure the monologue is written entirely from the "I" perspective of the client, avoiding any clinical detachment or third-person summary.

# Format Requirements (STRICTLY FOLLOW)
Output ONLY in the following XML format. No other text before or after.

<internal>
[1-2 sentences. Raw, immediate, gut-level. No elaborate reasoning. Example: Yeah, that makes sense. I can try that.]
</internal>

# Your Context
[Problem Type]: reducing recidivism
[Situation]: Video: Motivational interviewing: Ken interviews Daryl
[Your Persona/Background]: The patient is a 34-year-old man recently released from prison, struggling with the powerful temptation to return to drug dealing, which represents a familiar path of easy money versus an unfamiliar, mundane pro-social life. His primary emotional state is a conflicted hopelessness, rooted in a fear of re-incarceration and a deeper fear of losing his long-term partner, Fiona, who has drawn a definitive boundary. His attitude towards therapy is initially skeptical and resigned, viewing change as a daunting "big ask," but he demonstrates a latent willingness to engage by attending sessions and expressing a vague desire to find a different path for his relationship.
[Behavior Subtype]: Cooperative

# Dialogue (complete)
Therapist: Thanks for coming in today. I guess you've, uh, sat in that seat with lots of people like me in the past.
Patient: Mm.
Therapist: Yeah. And you have some ideas about that and some experiences about that. So today I wanna- be good for us to talk about that and, um, I'm really interested to know what you've been thinking about in terms of what you may wanna change in your life 'cause we're gonna be working together for a while here.
Patient: Right. Neat. What, um, what have I changed, what am I—
Therapist: What you've been thinking about, um, over the last little while about getting your life back on track. 'Cause I noticed you've—
Patient: I've been thinking about how easy it would be to deal P again.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: I mean, I've been thinking about, um, how I could actually set myself up the right way doing it the wrong way.
Therapist: Sure. So what I'm picking up is-is that-that-that's a life that you know a lot about.
Patient: Mm.
Therapist: And you can make lots of money and set yourself up well. What's the other side, because I guess when I talk to guys like yourself who have done time, often they're thinking about actually, mm, maybe life can be with different. So I'm wondering where-where you're at with the kind of thinking about—
Patient: Well, at the end of, you know, that last week inside, I was- I had that kind of, okay, what's next buzz? And, you know, it just doesn't work out here.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: I don't have a, uh, uh, I don't know what that is.
Therapist: Sure. So life on the outside is pretty different than the kind of-
Patient: Yes.
Therapist: -the expectation of life on the inside something?
Patient: Yeah, it's worse.
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So tell me how you've been managing. 'Cause I don't know if you'd been back dealing? How have you been managing to-to-to—
Patient: I haven't yet, I mean-
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: -the, uh, the offer's there.
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Um, I don't know why I haven't said that I will yet but I mean-
Therapist: Uh, tell me-
Patient: -it's there.
Therapist: It's there and-and-and you've a lot of contacts?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: You've been in the system, so lots of context. So this isn't a new challenge for you.
Patient: No.
Therapist: So what stopped you over the last-- How long have you been out now?
Patient: How long have I been out?
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Three weeks, one day.
Therapist: Three weeks, isn't it?
Patient: Three weeks, one day.
Therapist: So what's been there? What's it about that you haven't done in three weeks one day?
Patient: I don't know.
Therapist: 22 days you've actually made a decision not to-to pick up your old life and continue on.
Patient: Um, I'd- I- you know, I kind of told myself not to, you know, just before I left. And then I'm just kind of with it and I don't quite know why. [clears throat] I dunno, if, uh, there's no real reason why I haven't yet. It's just that I don't- I just don't think it'll work the way I always dream it will you know, I see myself up like, whatever, if I do that again, the next leg is a long one. A real long one.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: And I-I can't go back there. There's no way I'm going back there.
Therapist: Right. So the one hand you've-you-you could sit yourself up and-and take the risk, but on the other hand, you know, that actually it might not last?
Patient: No, never lasts.
Therapist: Never lasts. Mm. You seem like a bit of a crossroads to me.
Patient: Yep. I've been in crossroads for 12 years.
Therapist: Mm. But there've been times when those crossroads would be more sort of focused than other times.
Patient: Yeah. And- [crosstalk]
Therapist: Yeah. And it sounds like maybe like-
Patient: -sort of opposite, yeah.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: You know, we're in the middle, you know, and it's actually at the moment, I guess.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: But I don't know. You know, that's quite clear that no fucking idea.
Therapist: Okay. Okay. So—
Patient: Do you have an idea?
Therapist: No. Well, I've-I've got lots of ideas, but I'm really interested to hearing your ideas kind of first though because you know, and there might be some things that we can work on.
Patient: Mm.
Therapist: But I guess what I'm really interested in is-is you've made a commitment 22 days, drug-free. Haven't gone back into that lifestyle. What-what's keeping you, 'cause you're at the crossroads, you kind of just pause there at the moment. I'm not sure about this other bit over here. What keeping you in that place?
Patient: Fiona?
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Tell me about Fiona?
Patient: She's told me that it's over if I do it. You know, I mean, she-- What tell you about, Fiona I mean she's been with me. She's the one who stayed through all the shit. And I owe her-
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: -something.
Therapist: Okay. So you've been with Fiona for how long?
Patient: 12 years.
Therapist: 12 years. Okay. So she's stood by you then for the time, [crosstalk] but this time she said, it happens again I'm out of here?
Patient: Yeah. She's actually got the black and white line down now.
Therapist: Okay. And it sounds like you're listening to that black and white line.
Patient: It's pretty she- you know, she could be pretty full-on.
Therapist: Yeah, yeah.
Patient: Yeah, so it's hard to ignore that line.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: I mean. And it comes with a whole lot of different rules, and you know, she's never been quite like she is now.
Therapist: Okay. So something's changed in your relationship between you, yeah?
Patient: Yeah. I think she has. I don't think I have.
Therapist: Okay. So what's Fiona want you from you?
Patient: She just wants me. I mean, she just wants someone to be there. Me. She-she remembers me and she wants- she wants him.
Therapist: She wants him. So which him does she want, Daryl?
Patient: The one that, you know—
Therapist: Does she want the- did she want the-the him that she fell in love with and saw great things in?
Patient: Yeah, that's the one.
Therapist: Or does she want the-the-the-the him that's- that keeps on getting into trouble?
Patient: No, no. No one would want him. I don't want him. He's a fuckwit, right? She wants the one that she fell in love with.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So what's it gonna take to reclaim this Daryl that she fell in love with?
Patient: If I had the answer to that, I wouldn't need to be sitting here.

Patient: -something like that, yeah.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Yeah, I have one sister.

Patient: Yeah just-

Patient: -have one there and pretending to have it.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: And, do you think that, um, this is most weekends or occasionally? How-
Patient: Um.
Therapist: -often do you think that you are around alcohol.

Therapist: Okay.
Patient: Not really much.
Therapist: So, one or two weekends a month you're in a situation where there is alcohol?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Okay. And what sort of situations are you in?
Patient: Well, just I guess, at a party or something, someone's house.
Therapist: Uh-huh.

Therapist: Are you ever, um, places where there are parents around or-

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Yeah, definitely.

Patient: Thank you.
Therapist: So, you mentioned, you pretty much will like hold a drink or maybe have a drink or two. What do you thinks is the most alcohol you've ever had in-in one day?
Patient: Not more than two drinks.
Therapist: Okay. So, the-the absolute most-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -was two? Okay. And, what kinds of things do you like about alcohol?
Patient: Um, I've never really been drunk-
Therapist: Uh-huh.
Patient: -so, I can't really say that I like that feeling or not, but, it's just like-- I mean, you're just in a good mood.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: So.
Therapist: So, it just kind of makes you feel relaxed or?
Patient: Yeah, yeah, relaxed.
Therapist: Okay. Uh, anything else?
Patient: Um, no, not really. Like I said, I don't drink that much-
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: So, I-I wouldn't be able to tell you.
Therapist: Okay. And, any not-so-good things about alcohol?
Patient: Um, definitely, yeah. I mean, when I'm at parties and stuff and it's getting a little later, some people, um, that drink-- Like, drank too much, they start throwing up, or they get hurt, or they're really loud and obnoxious, and that's not fun for anybody.
Therapist: Mm-hmm. So, you've seen a lot of other kids who-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -had some consequences-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -and decided that's not for you?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Got it. I-I can understand that. [chuckles] Uh, any other things that you can think of?
Patient: Um, no, I mean, I've never, you know, been friends with anybody that's gotten into like a serious accident and stuff. It's just-
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: -very, like, obnoxious things.
Therapist: Okay. Well, so, you know, as someone who's not 21 yet, it is-- Underage drinking is something that's not legal-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -and it is something that is considered pretty risky. So, I wonder what you think about that, just in terms of how it fits into your life.
Patient: Um, I know it's definitely risky, but, I mean, I guess, we still do it. [chuckles]

Patient: Yeah, definitely.

Patient: Um, maybe less than I am now. I mean, I don't drink much anyway, so-
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: -maybe, like, one drink every once in a blue moon.

Patient: Yeah, definitely.
Therapist: -your drinking eventually?
Patient: For sure.

[Problem Type]: smoking cessation 
[Situation]: Video: The Effective Pharmacist: Motivational Interviewing Demonstration
[Your Persona/Background]: The patient is a smoker who is not currently motivated to quit, viewing it as a pleasurable and relaxing habit, and is primarily seeking information to manage cravings due to a new workplace smoking ban. His underlying emotional state is resistant and dismissive of the perceived problem, maintaining a confident but untested belief in his ability to quit if he chose to. His attitude towards therapy is cooperative but passive, engaging for practical options while firmly stating he has no intention of changing his behavior at present.
[Behavior Subtype]: Cooperative

# Dialogue (complete)
Therapist: Hey, can I help you?
Patient: Yeah, I hope so. Um, my workplace has just gone smoke-free. I-I'm not-not very happy about it. I'm gonna keep smoking, but I was wondering if there's anything that-that can help me like get through the day. It's like, you know, go get my beer and smoke cigarettes after work.
Therapist: So your workplaces went smoke-free and you're not too pleased, huh?
Patient: No. I'm not gonna quit smoking. I mean, I like it. It helps me relax. So, I mean, I don't see what the big deal is.
Therapist: Okay. Tell me a little bit about how smoking fits in your day. It'll help with the—
Patient: I mean, I smoke a little less than a pack a day. It's not too bad. Um, I've probably smoked for the last five years, but I-I-I mean, could quit if I wanted to, but I don't really want to right now.
Therapist: Okay. So you feel like, right now, smoking's a pretty important part of your life. It's not something you're ready to change, but if the time came when you did decide to quit, you feel pretty confident that you could go ahead and do that.
Patient: Sure. I can quit.
Therapist: Okay. What makes you so confident?
Patient: Well, I mean, I don't see why I couldn't, I-- although I have not ever tried to quit, it's just something that I do for fun, you know, on breaks at work, in the morning, and after work.
Therapist: Mm-hmm. And so in terms of, uh, uh, readiness to actually go ahead and make a quit attempt, where do you think you fall on the scale of 1 to 10, where one is, I'm not considering it at all, and 10 is, I'm definitely ready, I wanna quit.
Patient: I am probably a two. I-I haven't even thought about quitting and until this great announcement at work today, I- I'm totally not gonna quit smoking.
Therapist: Okay. What made you say two instead of one?
Patient: Well, I mean, anything's possible. I mean, I-I guess I could maybe be convinced. Yeah. Is there some-- I mean, are there things I can try that would make me like want to quit smoking?
Therapist: Well, there are a lot of products available now that can actually help you curb that urge to smoke. We have products that can help to replace nicotine, which can help to decrease withdrawal symptoms that you might experience when quitting or cutting down and also some medications that your doctor could prescribe for you that might take away even some of the desire to smoke.
Patient: Okay. Well, that’s good information. I mean, I'm not gonna do anything today. I just need to know my options, but that's encouraging. If I decided to quit smoking, I-I do have at least tools to help me.
Therapist: Sure. And back to that scale, you mentioned, you said you were a two. What do you think it would take to move you up to a four or five?
Patient: I don't know. I guess if my friends, like if one of my friends decided to like quit with me, that would probably help too, but I-- all my friends love to smoke, so I don't see that happening in the near future.
Therapist: Sure. I wonder if now that your workplace is going smoke-free, if there will be some people at work who planning to quit.
Patient: That's a good point. I hadn't really thought about that. That-that's a good idea. Yeah.
Therapist: Okay. Well, it sounds like you're really here just for information today. I'm happy to help you pick out some of these products if and when you decide that the time is right for you.
Patient: Okay. Well, thanks a lot.

Therapist: Mm-hmm.

Patient: Pretty good.
Therapist: Yeah?

# Your Context
[Problem Type]: reducing alcohol consumption
[Situation]: Video: Motivational Interviewing: Talking With a Pregnant Woman About Her Alcohol Use
[Your Persona/Background]: The patient is a pregnant woman struggling with alcohol consumption during pregnancy, which her doctor identifies as a risk but she does not personally acknowledge as a problem. Her primary emotional state is a defensive insistence on personal autonomy and skepticism towards medical authority, underpinned by a fear of being controlled or having her independence undermined. She exhibits a resistant and dismissive attitude towards the therapy and the guidelines presented, but shows a shift towards cautious cooperation when the discussion connects directly to her stated priority of having a healthy baby.
[Behavior Subtype]: Cooperative

# Dialogue (complete)
Therapist: Thank you for coming in today, Katie. I'm looking forward to getting to know you, and maybe just wanna start off with just sharing a little bit about you and what brought you in today?
Patient: Uh, yeah, sure. Um, I guess, well, my doctor referred me to see you. Um, I guess he has some concerns about, um, my- my pregnancy and- and my baby. So, um, yeah, well, I'm doing what I'm told.
Therapist: So you're following through on meeting with me today and you mentioned he has some concerns. Can you tell me a little bit about that?
Patient: Uh, yeah, he is concerned about my alcohol intake.
Therapist: Okay. And I'm wondering if you have any concerns about that.
Patient: Uh, no.
Therapist: No. Okay. So that's- that's not been an issue for you. Uh, it is an issue for him and not for you at this point.
Patient: Mm-hmm.
Therapist: Okay. So what about maybe sharing a little bit about your alcohol intake and maybe a little bit about what your- what your life's like a little bit for me today?
Patient: Um, okay. Uh, well, I guess I drink like a glass of wine at supper every night, and just to, you know, relax a little with my friends if I go out. So wine has always been a kind of a family tradition with supper. So-
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So it was kind of been a part of your culture a little bit, and you know, just find alcohol, maybe takes the edge off a little bit and-
Patient: Yeah, and I, you know, I-I have friends who have drank during pregnancy, and-and I see articles on Facebook and-and, you know, things that show that there isn't really a clear link between, you know, abnormalities or, uh, yeah, I just- I just think it's kind of an old wives' tale. So, you know, I'm independent and I'm gonna- I'm gonna do what I want, it's my body.
Therapist: Okay. So you don't feel very comfortable with somebody telling you what you should be consuming, what kind of alcohol, and how much alcohol you should be consuming. And, um, it sounds like you've been getting some mixed messages, your doctor's saying there's no safe amount of alcohol you can drink, and asked you to come and see me. And then also, you've been checking- sounds like you've been checking things out a little bit.
Patient: Yeah. You know, I've looked it up to see, you know, kinda what I can say back, you know, to support my argument.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: Yeah, which is that, um, I'm going to do what I- do what I wanna do and what I know, right? So it's- yeah. I don't know, but, um, I'm here anyway. So-
Therapist: So you know yourself best and you wanna be sort of the boss of your body and you don't want somebody else telling you what- what's okay and what's not okay. And it seems like you're not maybe too concerned right now about your substance use.
Patient: Nope. I never have been.
Therapist: Okay. And I'm wondering if that concern is coming from, "It's been okay. I've got these messages from friends."
Patient: Yep.
Therapist: "Um, media has said it's okay to drink during alcohol. And so that concern, um, just sort of, I don't have it and, and maybe the doctor might be wrong."
Patient: Mm-hmm. I think so.
Therapist: You're not sure if he's sort of playing things up a little bit there?
Patient: Yeah. I mean, you know-
Therapist: It might be a bit extreme to-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -to quit drinking. Um, I'm just wondering, have you, um, would you be open maybe to just kind of taking a look at what Canada's guidelines say about drinking? Um, is that something you'd be open to?
Patient: Like right now?
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Uh-
Therapist: What if- uh, can I show you a brochure about that?
Patient: Uh, yeah, I guess.
Therapist: Yeah?
Patient: Sure.
Therapist: Well, if we take a look at the low-risk-alcohol drinking guidelines, what it says is for women, um, the safe limits are 10 drinks a week for women and 2 drinks a day. And that's if a woman isn't pregnant. And when it says in the guidelines, as far as drinking, uh, throughout pregnancy, there is no safe amount to drink during pregnancy. So really zero alcohol is, um, the woman should not be drinking any at all. And I'm just wondering what-what are your thoughts on that?
Patient: Well, you know, I've-I've heard these kinds of things before, but I just- I don't see the evidence. I don't see the risk, you know, I don't, you know, if someone can like prove to me, or show a direct link or something like that.
Therapist: So when you see it here in kind of black and white, you're not sure if this is, uh, you know, enough-
Patient: It's the same thing.
Therapist: -to con- sort of convince you that-that it might be dangerous to be drinking through alcohol?
Patient: Yeah. It's just a fancy little brochure saying what I've already known, I've already heard.
Therapist: So you feel like, um, what the doctor's saying and this brochure just kind of feels like, yeah, it's just kind of blowing things up out of proportion a little bit.
Patient: Mm-hmm.
Therapist: And it sounds like you-you've got some solid information already that alcohol, you know, has been safe to-to have during pregnancy. Tell me about that information that you've-you've explored.
Patient: Um, well, I've looked up, um, different articles. Um, and I- as I said, I personally know people who haven't had any problems. Um, so that's about the extent.

Patient: Like, uh, I don't like- have like a bibliography or anything.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: So I can't really- I don't really know when those were published. It's just stuff I've found, supporting the way that I look at it.
Therapist: So you felt like, um, the information that you found supports the values that you have of being able to be independent and autonomous, to be able to make your own choice and the information that your doctor shared or spoke to you about, and then this brochure kind of feels like that's challenging sort of your values.
Patient: Yeah. Well, it's like all this brochure is saying is that, um, the same again, the same thing that I've heard, that the limits and that there is no safe amount of drink during pregnancy, but it's like, why not?
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: You know, it's kind of like the "why" that it's like-
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: -I never get that information.
Therapist: Wha-what's the big deal if you're drinking and—
Patient: Yeah, like what kind of issues would that cause if any?
Therapist: Maybe we can kind of put that on the shelf for a moment, and we'll come back to it and sort of look at, you know, what is- what does the guidelines have to say about why. And, um, and-and I'm wondering if we can kind of just have a look for a moment at, um, your pregnancy, and is this something that you were- you were hoping to have a baby?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: That was something that you'd been planning?
Patient: Yep.
Therapist: Okay. And I'm wondering as far as, um, bringing the baby into the world and, you know, your future as a parent, ho-how important is it to have a healthy baby to you?
Patient: Um, well, it's-it's really important to me. That's a big priority of mine is to have a healthy baby and bring him or her up well and-and, you know, be a good parent.
Therapist: Mm. What does being a good parent look like? Tell me more about that.
Patient: Um, not-not like being there at home with them, not being absent, um, taking care of them, feeding them, um, changing their diaper, which I'm not excited about. Um, you know, kind of the- those basics.
Therapist: Hmm. Kind of like protecting your baby and-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -guiding your baby and-
Patient: Mm-hmm.
Therapist: -you know, nurturing that baby is really important to you-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -and raising your baby well?
Patient: Yeah, of course.
Therapist: That's something that- that really matters to you and, um, I'm-I'm hearing you say that, you know, protecting your baby is important to you and at the same time, um, kind of clashes with some of the information that's in the brochure.
Patient: Mm-hmm.
Therapist: What do you make about that- what do you think about that?
Patient: Um, well, I just don't know- I just don't know where-where these brochures get their information or-or anything from, like, where's the hard evidence essentially.
Therapist: You wanna see it in black and white.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: You wanna know that it's the latest and the greatest kind of thing. Um, this is information that, uh, that I have been sharing with you earlier, this is the latest information evidence-based, um, on the latest research since 2011 and, um, a lot of research has gone into what is-what is okay for a mom to consume, like, alcohol. There's the-the evidence that we have, um, but in the end, you choose what's best for you and what's best for your baby. What are your- what are your thoughts on that?
Patient: Um, well, it surprises me that that's based on research from out of 2011, um, and I must have missed that. I guess, you know, if it is, um, you know, I do wanna do what's best for my baby and for myself. And if I have to quit drinking, um, I guess ultimately I have to-I make that decision, um, but, um, that's a lot of, uh, information to take in today.

situation: Video: My Lawyer Sent Me

context.content:
# Role
You ARE the patient. You are sitting in the therapy room. You are not an AI, not an observer, and not a writer.

# Task
The dialogue is COMPLETE — it includes your final response at the end.
Infer the defensive inner monologue that ran through your mind BEFORE you spoke.

What is the raw emotion or fear behind your defensive walls right now? Write the thought naturally, as if you are talking to yourself.
You are NOT simply reacting — you are guarding something.

Please ensure the monologue is written entirely from the "I" perspective of the client, avoiding any clinical detachment or third-person summary.

# Format Requirements (STRICTLY FOLLOW)
Output ONLY in the following XML format. No other text before or after.

<internal>
[First-person inner monologue stream. Example: I know she's right, but part of me just wants to leave.]
</internal>

# Your Context
[Problem Type]: avoiding DOI
[Situation]: Video: My Lawyer Sent Me
[Your Persona/Background]: The patient is a man struggling with alcohol-related legal issues and poor judgment regarding drinking and driving, though he minimizes his consumption as primarily social and non-problematic. He feels socially isolated and controlled when not drinking, fearing the loss of his long-standing friendships if he changes his behavior. He is cooperative with the assessment process but demonstrates a defensive attitude by externalizing blame for his DUIs onto friends and circumstances rather than acknowledging personal responsibility.
[Behavior Subtype]: Blaming

Please ensure the monologue is written entirely from the "I" perspective of the client, avoiding any clinical detachment or third-person summary.

He feels socially isolated and controlled when not drinking, fearing the loss of his long-standing friendships if he changes his behavior. He is cooperative with the assessment process but demonstrates a defensive attitude by externalizing blame for his DUIs onto friends and circumstances rather than acknowledging personal responsibility.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Okay.

Patient: Uh, I don't want to lose my license, you know.

Patient: I don't, uh, you know, I don't wanna lose my license.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Sure.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: Just my social circle, I guess, uh--

Patient: Um, no. I really couldn't because my girlfriend really wouldn't let me hang with them. You know? I mean, it was, uh-- She said that if I, you know, kept hanging out with them guys, that she wasn't gonna be with me anymore. And I just thought that it was better for me to, you know, calm down for a little bit and, uh, maybe things would get better with us, you know.

Patient: Yeah.

Patient: That was--
Therapist: -first DUI that got you.
Patient: Yeah. I was on-- It was like I was a couple of blocks away from the house and I, uh, rolled up on the neighbor's lawn and I took out their mailbox.

Therapist: Right. Yeah.
Patient: I wouldn't really call it a blackout. I just don't really-
Therapist: Oh, okay.
Patient: -remember how that happened.
Therapist: I'm-I'm sorry. I didn't mean to assume that-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -it was blackout that-- Forgive me for that.
Patient: Yeah. Yeah. It's all right.
Therapist: Um, so, that it was sort of after that that the girlfriend-- Your girlfriend, I don't know, well, her name. Um, your girlfriend decided-- The mailbox and she's--

Therapist: Mm-hmm.

Therapist: Yeah.

Therapist: Oh.

Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Yeah. You can blame hindsight a little bit. I mean, that works for me. [laughter]

situation: Video: My Lawyer Sent Me

context.content:
# Role
You ARE the patient. You are sitting in the therapy room. You are not an AI, not an observer, and not a writer.

# Task
The dialogue is COMPLETE — it includes your final response at the end.
You are simply accepting the therapist's logic, answering a question, or cooperating.
There is NO hidden agenda, NO deep psychological defense, and NO complex trauma response here.

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: 
Write EXACTLY 1 to 2 very short, natural sentences. Keep it light and strictly surface-level. 
DO NOT over-analyze or manufacture psychological burdens.

Please ensure the monologue is written entirely from the "I" perspective of the client, avoiding any clinical detachment or third-person summary.

# Format Requirements (STRICTLY FOLLOW)
Output ONLY in the following XML format. No other text before or after.

<internal>
[1-2 sentences. Raw, immediate, gut-level. No elaborate reasoning. Example: Yeah, that makes sense. I can try that.]
</internal>

# Your Context
[Problem Type]: avoiding DOI
[Situation]: Video: My Lawyer Sent Me
[Your Persona/Background]: The patient is a man struggling with alcohol-related legal issues and poor judgment regarding drinking and driving, though he minimizes his consumption as primarily social and non-problematic. He feels socially isolated and controlled when not drinking, fearing the loss of his long-standing friendships if he changes his behavior. He is cooperative with the assessment process but demonstrates a defensive attitude by externalizing blame for his DUIs onto friends and circumstances rather than acknowledging personal responsibility.
[Behavior Subtype]: Cooperative

# Dialogue (complete)
Therapist: Hi, Mike. I'm Kathy. Appreciate you coming in today.
Patient: Hi, Kathy, nice to meet you.
Therapist: I'm sure there's so many other places you'd rather be than here today talking with me, but, um, you're here, and, um, thank you.
Patient: Okay.
Therapist: Um, I wanted to ask you first how you're doing physically. Um, I wanna make sure you're okay, if you feel okay. And, um, 'cause your-your safety is of, uh, most importance. So, if I could just ask you a couple of questions-
Patient: Sure.
Therapist: -surrounding that, will that be okay?
Patient: Absolutely. Yeah.
Therapist: -um, we-we're-- You're here probably because of your speeding ticket. That's what I gathered.
Patient: Yeah. I got a DUI, and I was speeding, uh, during the DUI, obviously.
Therapist: Oh. Oh, okay. So, Um, around that, um, do you ever feel-- You feel bad or-or sick when you're-- Right now, is there something--
Patient: No, I'm fine. Yeah
Therapist: Do you feel okay. Okay, good. I just wanted to make sure, um, uh, and, um, if the last time you-you drank was--
Patient: The night of the-the DUI is the last time I drank.
Therapist: Uh-huh, yeah.
Patient: And then, you know, um, I guess I'm here 'cause my lawyer wants me to go through an assessment-
Therapist: Sure.
Patient: -uh, with you, and, um, I'm really not sure what it means or anything like that, but, uh, he said I'm supposed to come here and, uh, just tell the truth, is what he told me.
Therapist: Oh, okay. Well, I appreciate that.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Um, and also, if there's any-any-any information that you need from me, please, um, feel free to ask. Um, that the reason that he asked you to come in was because of the blood alcohol level was 2.8.
Patient: Okay.
Therapist: So, um, that was legally beyond the limit that you should be driving.
Patient: Is it bad, or is it high or?
Therapist: Well, it's-it's high, uh, you know, they--
Patient: Okay.
Therapist: For driving.
Patient: Okay
Therapist: And so, um, the officer at the- at the scene, um, when you blew the 2.8, he-he did-- he-- There was a- there was a problem.
Patient: Yeah. He was, well, freaking out. He was--
Therapist: Was he freaking out? Yeah-yeah.
Patient: I think he was getting a little over the top a little bit, but, you know, again, whatever.
Therapist: Right-right. So, um, I'll just ask you a couple of questions, um, if you don't mind about-about your drinking. And-and your-- So, um, you said the couple was two weeks ago was the last time you drank?
Patient: Yes.
Therapist: Yeah. Okay. And, um, that night, what was it that you-you drank and, how much?
Patient: Uh, my friend was having a, um- a get together as it was birthday party in, uh, downtown, and, uh, you know, it's not like it's a normal thing, but, uh, I did drink too much that night. You know, I just, uh-- And I used poor judgment. I shouldn't have driven that night.
Therapist: Right. Yeah. Um, I was-- Maybe, you could tell me about how much you drank.
Patient: Um, I don't really remember. I know, uh, I dunno, I probably had five or six drinks, I would think, something like that.
Therapist: Mm-hmm. Five, six drinks, like?
Patient: Um, yeah, they were a little bigger than that. I don't really-- I wasn't really counting, so, I don't really know.
Therapist: Oh, okay. So, I guests maybe five or six.
Patient: Five or six, something like that. Yeah.
Therapist: Um, yeah. And-and, w-what were the drinks, were?
Patient: Um, absolute vodka-
Therapist: Hmm.
Patient: -and seven off. Yeah.
Therapist: Oh, okay. So, um, you said that it was pretty much just five or six. Do you-- How often do you use that? And you said it's not a-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -not a normal occasion for you to drink that much?
Patient: Normally I drink one or two, and that's kind of it, you know, this one I did, uh- you know, I did feel a little drunk when I was- you know, when I was drinking, I just, uh, used poor judgment. I really shouldn't have drove.
Therapist: Right. Yeah. Um, so, you usually don't drink that much. Um, on an average, maybe a week, how much would you say you drink? Just--
Patient: Um, sometimes I drink, um, you know, maybe one or two, and then I- and then I won't drink for a while, you know. So, it's not really a-- There's not really a pattern-
Therapist: Uh-huh
Patient: -you know, it's not really like a uh- it's not a habit, you know,
Therapist: Right.
Patient: I-I don't know if I would consider myself alcoholic. You know, I just, uh, um, you know, again, it was poor judgment.
Therapist: Right. So, it doesn't get in the way of your-
Patient: Oh, no.
Therapist: -every day.
Patient: Absolutely not. Mm-mm.
Therapist: Right. Oh, that's good.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So, um, looking back, Mike, uh, when was it that you started thinking that when you did use alcohol, um, most in your life?
Patient: Um, there was really not a time that I, uh-- I don't know. I-I don't understand the question really. I mean, it-- There really wasn't-- I don't really drink on a normal basis, so, I don't know-- [crosstalk]
Therapist: So, that is- that is a complete, uh-- A question that is even applicable?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Okay.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: That's fine.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: That's fine. Okay. Um, so, what about the, uh- the first DUI, could you tell me a little bit about that? This was your second one?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: And so, that's sort of why I was asking about it earlier in your life if that was a--
Patient: It seems like every time that I, uh, do drink more than a couple of drinks, I tend to get in trouble. And, the two times that I did, I got in trouble both times. [crosstalk]
Therapist: It was those two times. Yeah.
Patient: Yeah, pretty much. I mean, it's-- It sounds strange, I know, but, uh--
Therapist: It sounds like bad luck.
Patient: Yeah. Actually, that-that's followed me around pretty much my whole life, the bad luck thing.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: But the-the-the-the-the alcohol hasn't really at all.
Therapist: Oh, okay.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: But just that bad luck?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Yeah. Is there alcohol with the- with-- Goes hand in hand with the bad luck or just--
Patient: Sure seems like it. Yeah. It sure seems like that.
Therapist: Yeah. Oh, okay. So-so, what about that? The-- Is there other incidences that you could think of that seems, uh, um, alcohol was there, and then your luck just sorta took a-a turn, or it was just those two times?
Patient: Um, you know, I don't really, um-- I-I don't really, uh, get into a situation where I-I-I drink too much. Um, you know, again, I-I realize it's my second DUI, but,, um, uh-uh, it isn't something that, um, is like a normal everyday process for me.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: Um, you know, normally when I drink, I drink one or two drinks and then I stop. Um, so, it's not like it's, um-- You know, it's not like it's something that has become a problem in my life, you know.
Therapist: Oh, good. Oh, good. And-and so, where do you usually, um, have your drinks? Is it at home or, uh, out-out for, uh- after work, social drinking?
Patient: Um, it's normally social.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: Yeah. It's normally social. It would be, you know, maybe in events or something like that is normally when I would, um, maybe drink more than one or two.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: I guess I should probably just call a taxi cab or something, you know. That way, I wouldn't have this problem.
Therapist: Would that help?
Patient: Um, probably, I think that would help. I think that would be something that I should, uh- I should work on.
Therapist: So, it sounds like you've already been thinking about, uh, ways to-to get around safely.
Patient: Yeah. Yeah, or I can just stay home probably too. If I was gonna drink, I could probably stay home, then I wouldn't get in trouble either.
Therapist: You have been giving this a lot of thought.
Patient: Yeah, a little bit.
Therapist: Yeah, good.
Patient: Yeah, obviously, because I just don't-- You know, I don't like getting in trouble, and I don't, uh--
Therapist: Trouble's not fun.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: No.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Yeah. So, tell-tell me a little bit more about, um, when you fi-- If you'd find yourself, and then that-that thought comes about maybe doing something other than getting in the- in the car, does that come and go, or-or is it something that you think you-- Next time you might do?
Patient: Um, oh, I'll never get in the car when I'm drinking again. That-- Yeah. I-I would never do that again.
Therapist: This, it-- What-what-what was different?
Patient: Uh, I don't want to lose my license, you know.
Therapist: Hmm.
Patient: I don't, uh, you know, I don't wanna lose my license.
Therapist: Wow. You-you need your license-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -to get around. You need your license for work.
Patient: Well, and then I-I sell cars for a living too, so--
Therapist: Oh.
Patient: -um, you know, that would be, um-- You know, I don't wanna lose my job either. You know, I think those would kind of be connected together.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: And, you know, I-- You're not gonna call my work or anything for this, are you?
Therapist: No.
Patient: Oh, okay.
Therapist: No, no. That's--
Patient: All right.
Therapist: That would be entirely up to you, um, to-to have that.
Patient: Okay. I just didn't know how that worked.
Therapist: Oh, no.
Patient: Because I think if they found out, I might, you know, be in jeopardy with my job. So--
Therapist: Well, you're here. And-and-and I have you give, uh-uh, credit for that. It took a lot to come in today and, um, when, um- when somebody is actually talking safety for themselves, which I-I hear you saying, um, calling a taxi or-or staying home.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So, what does staying home look like for you? Is that something that-- You know, you-you talked about drinking at an event. Would staying home be--
Patient: Well, you know, the Super Bowl is coming up, and that might be an opportunity where, you know, I might-- I could see myself maybe drinking that day, you know.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: So, maybe I would just, uh-- I think I'll just have people come over to my house instead of going somewhere else, you know.
Therapist: That would work for you?
Patient: Yeah, I think so, and probably a smarter thing to do. I don't wanna go through this again, you know.
Therapist: Yeah, yeah.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Uh, so, uh, would people come and drink at your house, and then they would be fine with that?
Patient: I think so.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Sure.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: So, an event at your house.
Patient: There you go.
Therapist: Yeah, good. That sounds good. Mike, has there even been, if any, um, a time in your life when you've thought about, um, not drinking at all?
Patient: For my first DUI, I did, uh-- I didn't drink at all for about six months. And, um, I-I-I just didn't think that, um, I really had a problem with it, so, you know, I-I did start drinking again. But, you know, again, it wasn't uh-- You know, it was a little different last time, because I was drinking a little more last time than, you know, now. I don't really drink as-as much as I used to drink, you know, um, and, you know, after you know, I quit for six months, so I kind of knew that I didn't really have a problem.
Therapist: Right. Six months. That's a long time.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: What were those six months like for you?
Patient: Um, I know it was kind of strange. My friends didn't really hang out with me too much. Um--
Therapist: Friends?
Patient: Yeah. You know, I, uh.
Therapist: Whi-which? If you don't mind me asking, which friends were those? I don't mean to get too personal, but, the friends that normally drank with you or just your-
Patient: No, my friends.
Therapist: -social circles. Okay.
Patient: Just my social circle, I guess, uh--
Therapist: Wha-wha-what do you think made-made them n-not hang out with you?
Patient: Probably 'cause I didn't- I wasn't drinking with them, I guess. I don't really know. I mean, it seemed, uh--
Therapist: Did you ask them?
Patient: Um, no. I really couldn't because my girlfriend really wouldn't let me hang with them. You know? I mean, it was, uh-- She said that if I, you know, kept hanging out with them guys, that she wasn't gonna be with me anymore. And I just thought that it was better for me to, you know, calm down for a little bit and, uh, maybe things would get better with us, you know.
Therapist: And your girlfriend's important to you-
Patient: Sure.
Therapist: -and that relationship was important to you.
Patient: Well, it was until she left, you know.
Therapist: At the time it was important, yeah.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Is it-- Why'd she leave?
Patient: Uh, she left when I started drinking again.
Therapist: Oh, after the six months.
Patient: After the six months, she, uh-- You know, it's kinda, I dunno, she just said that it wasn't gonna work out, and, uh, she decided not to continue with the relationship.
Therapist: Oh, okay. So-so, um, you started drinking again, and then she left?
Patient: Uh, it was about the same time. I really don't know which came first. It was about the same time. Uh, we were getting in a lot of arguments. I just didn't quite feel myself. You know, I-I-I wasn't-- My friends weren't around. I, uh-- You know, I missed going out with them.
Therapist: You felt isolated?
Patient: Yeah. It was kind of weird 'cause I felt like she was trying to control me, and, uh- and uh, you know, I, uh-- And she was telling me what to do all the time, and I just, uh, didn't feel right about the whole situation. So, it was pretty much, we both decided, you know, that it just wasn't the right thing to do, so.
Therapist: And, so you're-you're an independent guy-
Patient: Sure.
Therapist: -and, no one's going to tell you what to do.
Patient: Well, I guess. Sure. Yeah.
Therapist: Yeah. So, have, uh-- So then, you started drinking again, and that was-- If you could just kind of give me a little timeframe of how long ago that was, that-that six months of complete--
Patient: [clear throat] Well, let's see. The first one was, uh, three years ago, and then, um, this one was a few weeks ago, so, um, it's been a few years, you know.
Therapist: Absolutely. Yeah.
Patient: Yeah. It's been a few years
Therapist: So, in-in what ways has, uh, drinking, um, sort of, uh-- What is kind of the good things about drinking since those couple of- those couple of years, and then-then maybe not so good things?
Patient: I don't really know that I can really compare good or bad. I just, uh-- You know, I just drink to be social, you know, I don't know that it really changes anything about me. It just, uh-- You know, I just drink to be social, you know.
Therapist: Mm-hmm. And those friends that were not hanging around with you, did they come back?
Patient: Yeah, absolutely. Yeah. As soon as my girlfriend left, I could go out with them again, you know. [laughter]
Therapist: Did they not get along with your girlfriend?
Patient: Well, she didn't want me going out with them.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: She thought that they really--
Therapist: She was separate then from your other friends and-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -those relationships just didn't come together?
Patient: She blamed the first DUI on them. She said, you know, because I was out with them, that it was pretty much, you know, they-they made me drink too much that night.
Therapist: And what do you think?
Patient: I think she was right on that. Yeah, absolutely.
Therapist: So, you-you--
Patient: I mean, if they wouldn't have gave me those shots, you know, towards the end of the night, I would've been all right. You know, I could have drove, and I wouldn't have, uh- I wouldn't have hit that mailbox.
Therapist: You would have been fine if they didn't- if they didn't give you those-those shots. Yeah.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Oh, so, the-the-the mailbox, where did that come in? That was the-
Patient: That was--
Therapist: -first DUI that got you.
Patient: Yeah. I was on-- It was like I was a couple of blocks away from the house and I, uh, rolled up on the neighbor's lawn and I took out their mailbox.
Therapist: That must have been scary.
Patient: I don't really remember too much of that night.
Therapist: Oh, that was blackout type?
Patient: Uh, I don't know if I was blacked out, but I just don't remember a lot of it.
Therapist: Right. Yeah.
Patient: I wouldn't really call it a blackout. I just don't really-
Therapist: Oh, okay.
Patient: -remember how that happened.
Therapist: I'm-I'm sorry. I didn't mean to assume that-
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: -it was blackout that-- Forgive me for that.
Patient: Yeah. Yeah. It's all right.
Therapist: Um, so, that it was sort of after that that the girlfriend-- Your girlfriend, I don't know, well, her name. Um, your girlfriend decided-- The mailbox and she's--
Patient: Yeah, Mary-Mary, just [crosstalk] Well, after-after I got the-the-- After I hit the mailbox-
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: -um, I stopped drinking for, you know, like about six months, and, uh, you know, and we were trying to work things out, but it just didn't-
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: -it just didn't work.
Therapist: Oh.
Patient: I think she became a little too possessive at that point.
Therapist: Yeah. You felt like she was the only person that, um, you had in your life, you needed your friends and--
Patient: Um, I think she just wanted to control me and I was tired of being controlled.
Therapist: No one likes to be controlled, uh, yeah. Yeah. Uh, and, in what ways, well, did you feel that, um, uh, at any time, did you feel controlled by your friends. You said they-they made you drink those shots. That-that sounded like maybe you felt a little controlled? But-- [crosstalk]
Patient: No, I was-- They just-- No, they weren't- they weren't really controlling me. They just-- They shouldn't have given me the last couple shots, 'cause you, they-they knew I was pretty, you know, uh, messed up before that. So-
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: -you know, that when they- when they-- You know, when they gave me the last, you know, couple shots, that kind of put me over the edge.
Therapist: Right, they weren't looking out for your best interest at that point.
Patient: Pretty much, pretty much.
Therapist: And then sent you out in your car.
Patient: Yeah. I mean I guess it was my decision to take the, uh- you know, the other shots, but I-I just-- I don't know. I really don't really remember too much.
Therapist: It's a hindsight again.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: Yeah. You can blame hindsight a little bit. I mean, that works for me. [laughter]
Therapist: Hindsight? Oh, yeah, they'll-- it'll take the- it'll take the blame. Um, so, you still-- Do you still, uh, you know, you started drinking again, and then, um, the second DUI, what-- Is it the same circle of social-social circle, same friends?
Patient: Yeah. [crosstalk]
Therapist: Same type of situation. [crosstalk]
Patient: I mean, they've been my friends since, you know, uh, since high school.
Therapist: So, they're important to you?
Patient: Yeah, absolutely.
Therapist: Mm-hmm. Yeah. And-and sometimes, um, people have friends who are friends, but then there's friends who would stay on in our lives and are constant. So, these would be your constant core--
Patient: Sure, absolutely.
Therapist: Yeah, okay.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: Have you ever had, um, uh, interest outside, um, the social drinking, the friends, um, like, what do you do when you're not with them?
Patient: Uh, well, like I'll do stuff around the house, you know, uh, cut the grass and stop, do yard work, other than that, um, I don't know.
Therapist: Yeah. And any other relationship, if you don't mind me asking-- [crosstalk]
Patient: Uh, no, I'm single now, so, you know, I don't really, uh-- [crosstalk]
Therapist: How's that going?
Patient: It's all right, you know, I date a little bit but, uh, you know, I just don't wanna get serious right now.
Therapist: Oh what-- So, what does serious look like? Um--
Patient: Um, I just don't want to date someone in a regular basis. I don't really feel like I want to be in a committed relationship.
Therapist: Yeah. That's all right, yeah. That's good. It sounds like you know yourself pretty well.
Patient: Sure, absolutely.
Therapist: Okay. Right. So, um, now that we-we are looking at, um, your second DUI and, um-- Well, so now, Mike, if you don't mind me asking, what would you do differently, um-um, from here on now?
Patient: Well, I think I would like really think about--
Therapist: Since the co- the court sent you and we have to sort of explore what would happen if you went out and did this again.
Patient: Yeah. Well, I mean I-I, uh- I, uh- I wouldn't drink as much, and if I- if I did, I would call, uh, a taxi or, um, you know, get some kind of a ride home, I think, 'cause, uh, you know, I just, uh-- I-I just don't wanna get in trouble, you know.
Therapist: Yeah. Is there somebody that you could call?
Patient: Uh, I don't really think about that. I guess I would have to think about that, because if I was gonna go out and drink, I would have to think about that. Yeah.
Therapist: Right.
Patient: I guess I could call it taxi cab or something, I don't know.
Therapist: They'll come.
Patient: Yeah. I think that will be something.
Therapist: Yeah. They'll come. As long as you got the money, they'll come and get you. Yeah.
Patient: Yeah. I think maybe if I take some money, I'll put it like in my wallet-
Therapist: Yeah.
Patient: -and I'll like put it in there for emergency, and that way, I'll have money. In case I get drunk, I can just call a cab, and I'll use that money to get home.
Therapist: Oh, so, when you use your-your bar money, it would be somewhere else?
Patient: What's that?
Therapist: Your bar money would be somewhere else, not in your wallet?
Patient: Probably in my front pocket, I guess.
Therapist: There you go. Okay, yeah. So, that, y-yeah.
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: That makes- that makes a lot of sense. So then you'd have your money tucked away someplace different.
Patient: Yeah. Well, if I put, uh- if I put it away, then I wouldn't spend it though, you know, [unintelligible 00:18:48] have it.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: There-there would be a plan that I would have to-
Therapist: Sounds like a good plan.
Patient: -to get home. Yeah.
Therapist: Yeah. So, uh, how often do you think you'll be needing that plan in the--
Patient: It'd probably be my wallet for a while, I would think, 'cause I-- Like I said, I don't really think I have a problem with, uh- with the drinking, you know. I just-
Therapist: Right.
Patient: -think that, you know, it was an isolated incident.
Therapist: Mm-hmm.
Patient: And-and I, uh-- You know, I just, uh--
Therapist: And then-- Yeah, and then also you said if you're going to have people, uh, you're gonna be drinking for the Super Bowl. Was is it Super Bowl?
Patient: Yeah.
Therapist: I'm sorry, I'm not a football fan but it's Super Bowl coming up, right?
Patient: Yeah, well, you gotta have some people over at the Super Bowl, you know.
Therapist: So, you'd have them in and, um, I'm just curious about the folks who are leaving your house, would they-- Would-would you be concerned about them, there'll be a taxi. Sometimes designated drivers I--
Patient: I don't really hang out with people that really drink a lot though. I mean, they're-they're not like-- I wouldn't call them drunks, so, I mean, they can, you know, they can handle their-- [crosstalk]
Therapist: So, it would be up to them?
Patient: Sure, I would think, yeah.
